meta.title: nono
meta.subject: nono - a capability-based, policy-governed runtime for AI agents

scenes.title: nono
scenes.tagline: A capability-granting runtime for AI agents
scenes.footnote: Rust   ·   four crates   ·   ~116k lines traced at commit 9b54f4b

beats.narration: nono is a runtime for letting an agent work in a real repository without handing it the whole machine.

It lives between full host trust and a guest operating system. The process stays on the host, but every path, host, socket, environment variable, and credential is supposed to become an explicit capability.
What matters is not the slogan but the seam. This review is about where nono keeps mechanism, where it keeps policy, and what that choice costs once the happy path ends.

scenes.heading: Mechanism in the core, policy in the runtime

beats.narration: The repo is only four workspace members, but one seam dominates the architecture.
The command-line runtime owns policy. Profiles, packs, trust scanning, network resolution, audit, rollback, and sessions all terminate in nono-cli.

The core library below it is intentionally policy-free. It applies exactly the capability set a caller hands it, and nothing more. That makes it reusable, but it also means safety does not come from the library by default.
The proxy is a separate crate because network control and credential isolation are not just another library call. Once proxy mode is active, this process becomes part of the trusted computing base.
That same split is why the repo can ship a C binding without re-arguing policy in another language. Callers reuse the core; policy still stays above it.
And the actual boundary is still the operating system: Landlock on Linux, Seatbelt on macOS. The library drives them; the kernel enforces them.

scenes.heading: The launch path turns policy into runtime data

beats.narration: Before any child exists, nono-cli compiles several kinds of policy into runtime data. The output is a launch plan, not a live negotiation with the sandboxed process.
Profiles are the first input. They can extend other profiles left to right, up to 10 levels deep, and head just added profile-level target binaries. This is still an active seam in the codebase.
Network policy is resolved separately. Named groups flatten into exact hosts, suffix matches, and credential routes; custom credentials can override built-ins before the proxy ever starts.

Trust is also compiled before exec. Instruction files are scanned, counted, and either admitted or blocked. Verified files are then write-protected and folded back into the capability set as system-owned reads.

Only then does the launch plan close over the program, the capability set, the proxy configuration, and the session and rollback flags. After this point the runtime is executing a compiled policy, not improvising one.

scenes.heading: Trust verification is on the launch path, not beside it

beats.narration: This is the load-bearing trust gate. It sits on the launch path, before the child exists.
The function loads the scan policy, runs the pre-exec scan, and keeps exact counts of verified, blocked, and warned files. Trust here is not a side report; it is part of launch planning.
The decision point is hard fail. If the result says do not proceed, launch returns a trust-verification error and the run stops before any agent can read those instruction files.
And a successful scan does not just bless the files conceptually. It turns them into concrete runtime state: the verified paths are collected and write-protected inside the capability set.

scenes.heading: A supervised run turns compiled policy into a sandboxed process

beats.narration: A real run starts by resolving the declared policy: profile inheritance, packs, network settings, and trust rules.
The compiler returns a concrete launch plan: program, capabilities, proxy options, and session settings.
By default the runtime stays supervised. The parent remains unsandboxed so it can own audit, rollback, attach, approvals, and proxy mode.
Before the child even exists, the parent creates the session file and terminal relay, and may start rollback baseline work because detached startup has a real timeout window.
The child is forked and then applies the sandbox itself. On Linux the order matters: Landlock first, seccomp notify after, or the child deadlocks during setup.
If the child opens a path outside its initial set on Linux, the request traps back to the parent. The parent re-resolves the path, checks protected roots, rate-limits prompts, and can still deny.
Approval does not let the child's original syscall continue. The parent opens the file itself and passes back the descriptor, binding the decision to the object it actually opened.
Network egress takes a parallel route. In proxy-only mode the child reaches the outside world only through the local proxy, carrying a session token instead of the real credential.

scenes.heading: The trusted parent is the real control plane

beats.narration: On Linux, on-demand access is not a magical kernel exception. The child asks the parent over a unix socket whose messages are capped at 64 kilobytes, and the parent answers with an opened file descriptor.

Terminal attachment lives beside that path. The supervisor owns the pseudo-terminal master, keeps up to eight mebibytes of scrollback, and exposes the attach socket; the child never gains a new capability just because a human reconnects.
Audit stays in the same trusted parent. The child can cause events, but it does not author its own trail; the parent writes the append-only stream, the chain head, and the Merkle summary.
Rollback is likewise parent-side and explicitly bounded. Baseline and incremental snapshots stop after 300,000 entries or 2 gigabytes instead of walking forever through a pathological tree.

And proxy mode makes the parent even heavier. A 2-thread runtime holds the real credentials, enforces route policy, and authenticates requests with a 256-bit session token. This is elegant, but it is also a concentration of trust.

scenes.heading: Failure modes and the alternatives not taken

nodes.label: Proxy trust boundary
nodes.sub: unsandboxed process sees secrets and plaintext

nodes.label: Allow-list tax
nodes.sub: broad profiles can turn policy into theatre

nodes.label: Same-host capability control
nodes.sub: fine-grained access in the real repo

nodes.label: Platform and W-S-L two skew
nodes.sub: Landlock, Seatbelt, and degraded modes differ

beats.narration: Here is the architecture's central bargain. nono chose same-host capability control over a guest operating system by default. That wins on working with the real repository and real tools. It costs a weaker outer boundary.

It also chose Landlock plus seccomp over making mount namespaces the baseline. That keeps the sandbox usable on unprivileged Linux installs. It costs information hiding, because stat and access reconnaissance still exist.
The sharpest single risk is the proxy boundary. The design keeps credentials out of the child by moving them into an unsandboxed process that still sees the decrypted traffic.

The softer but probably more common risk is authoring pressure. An allow-list fails closed, which is correct, but painful profiles invite users to widen them until the safety story becomes theatre. Platform and W-S-L two skew make that pressure worse.

So the verdict is conditional. For cooperative agents on a developer or C-I machine, this is a serious architecture. For hostile code or multi-tenant isolation, it needs an outer container or micro-v-m layer, not confidence alone.

scenes.heading: nono - an honest scorecard
points: Strong - policy is compiled in one place and applied by a policy-free core.
Strong - launch trust is real: instruction files are scanned before exec, not merely logged after the fact.
Costly - the allow-list and cross-platform authoring tax is permanent.
Fragile - the trusted parent and proxy concentrate audit, rollback, approvals, and credentials in one unsandboxed boundary.
Skip it - if you need hostile multi-tenant isolation or cannot trust the host kernel, put nono inside a container or micro-v-m.

beats.narration: First, the strongest part. Policy really is compiled in one place and then applied by a smaller, policy-free core.
Second, trust is not decorative. Instruction files are scanned before exec, and successful verification changes the capability set itself.
The price is permanent. An allow-list is the right security posture, but it creates an authoring tax and a platform-compatibility tax that the project never gets to stop paying.
The main fragility is also clear. The trusted parent and proxy concentrate approvals, audit, rollback, terminal relay, and credentials in one unsandboxed boundary.

So the honest scorecard is this: nono is a strong architecture for cooperative agents that need fine-grained control on a real host. It is not the end of the story for hostile code, multi-tenant isolation, or environments where the host kernel itself cannot be the thing you trust.
